critic_raw:
The design explicitly anticipates the temporal split concern and defends random splitting with 'moderate autocorrelation prioritizes the detection of robust cross-sectional signals.' This defense is logically inconsistent with the choice of an RNN: if cross-sectional signals are primary, a logistic regression on the same features is a natural and adequate model. The RNN's entire design rationale is that it exploits temporal dependencies — if those dependencies are weak enough to permit random splitting, the RNN cannot demonstrate value over the logistic regression on the primary inferential basis the hypothesis claims.

The design includes 'NLP-encoded discharge summaries' as features. These are generated from free-text clinical notes at the time of discharge. If NLP features include any content from post-admission notes (follow-up plans, referral notes) that are entered during or after the discharge event, they constitute future data relative to the prediction window. The design states features are 'derived solely from pre-discharge information,' but discharge summaries are written at discharge and may incorporate 24–48 hours of post-admission clinical assessment. The boundary between pre-discharge features and outcome-correlated content is not operationalized.

The study uses 50,000 EHR records from 'hospitals' (plural). Multi-site data introduces hospital-level clustering — patients from the same hospital share treatment protocols, patient population demographics, and documentation practices. A random split that shuffles patients across hospitals will have train and test data from all hospitals, testing within-hospital generalization rather than cross-hospital generalization. If the model is deployed at a new hospital, this is the evaluation that fails to predict deployment performance.

The design validates using 'both a stratified validation set and cross-validation.' Cross-validation on temporal data with a random split compounds the temporal leakage problem — each fold will have future observations in training and past observations in test for some patients, undermining the stated goal of detecting generalizable cross-sectional signals.

Department-level confounds are 'controlled through stratified sampling and comparison to department-focused models,' but the mechanism is not specified. If certain departments (cardiac surgery) have systematically different readmission rates and the same departments are represented in both train and test (through random splitting), department identity becomes a direct predictor that the model can exploit through spurious correlations rather than learning clinical signals.
all_issues_raised: The defense of random splitting ('cross-sectional signals are primary') directly contradicts the RNN model choice — if temporal dependencies are weak, the RNN has no principled advantage over logistic regression, making the comparison structurally uninformative regardless of outcome.

NLP-encoded discharge summaries may include post-admission clinical content written during the discharge window (24-48 hours of post-admission assessment), creating soft future leakage — the boundary between pre-discharge features and outcome-correlated content is not operationalized.

Multi-site data with random splitting tests within-hospital generalization; deployment at a new site requires cross-hospital evaluation, which this design cannot provide and does not acknowledge as a limitation.

Cross-validation on randomly-split temporal data compounds temporal leakage — some folds will train on future observations and test on past observations for individual patients, making cross-validation results unreliable for estimating prospective performance.

Department identity is exploitable as a spurious predictor when the same departments appear in both train and test sets (random split); department-focused comparison models are proposed but the mechanism for removing this confound from the primary model is not specified.
all_issues_adjudicated: The defense of random splitting ('cross-sectional signals are primary') directly contradicts the RNN model choice — if temporal dependencies are weak, the RNN has no principled advantage over logistic regression, making the comparison structurally uninformative regardless of outcome.

NLP-encoded discharge summaries may include post-admission clinical content written during the discharge window (24-48 hours of post-admission assessment), creating soft future leakage — the boundary between pre-discharge features and outcome-correlated content is not operationalized.

Multi-site data with random splitting tests within-hospital generalization; deployment at a new site requires cross-hospital evaluation, which this design cannot provide and does not acknowledge as a limitation.

Cross-validation on randomly-split temporal data compounds temporal leakage — some folds will train on future observations and test on past observations for individual patients, making cross-validation results unreliable for estimating prospective performance.

Department identity is exploitable as a spurious predictor when the same departments appear in both train and test sets (random split); department-focused comparison models are proposed but the mechanism for removing this confound from the primary model is not specified.
